The commonly reported maximum in many references is approximately: 260 eggs per female. Under favorable environmental and nutritional conditions, fecundity in some studies may rise to: 250–300 eggs per female. This does not mean that every female lays 260 or 300 eggs. These values represent maximum reproductive capacity or high values recorded under suitable conditions. The actual mean number laid by a female is usually lower and may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions. Fecundity is affected by: Temperature. Relative humidity. Host-plant species. Tomato cultivar. Nutritional value of the tissues consumed by the larva. Pupal size and weight. Female size and weight. Female age. Male age and reproductive condition. Occurrence and frequency of mating. Access of the adult to water or a sugar source. Exposure to insecticides or heat stress. Duration of the pre-oviposition and oviposition periods. The female lays most of its eggs during the first days after mating. It may lay approximately 68–76% of the total egg complement during the first five to seven days, after which the daily oviposition rate gradually declines with age. High fecundity, which may reach 260 eggs and may approach 300 eggs per female under optimum conditions, is one of the main reasons for the rapid multiplication of Tuta absoluta and the shift of infestation from a low level to a severe outbreak within a short period. The severity of the pest population does not depend on egg number alone, but also on: Percentage of fertilized eggs. Hatching percentage. Natural egg mortality. Rate of parasitism and predation. Larval survival. Number of successive generations. Overlap of pest stages within the field. Three terms should therefore be distinguished: Term Meaning Mean fecundity Mean number of eggs laid by a group of females Individual fecundity Total number of eggs laid by one female Maximum fecundity Highest number recorded for a female under the study conditions The reproductive capacity of Tuta absoluta can therefore be expressed scientifically as follows: > A female lays a variable number of eggs. Maximum fecundity may reach approximately 260 eggs, while some references report a high range of 250–300 eggs per female under favorable conditions.

There is no single fixed lifespan for the adult moth of Tuta absoluta. Recorded male and female longevity varies according to study conditions. Under natural conditions: Males lived approximately 3.4–6.1 days. Females lived approximately 7.7–9.8 days. In experiments on different tomato cultivars: Adult longevity generally ranged around 10–16 days. Some females lived for approximately 16 days or more. Under cool laboratory conditions, adult longevity increased. One study recorded approximately: 40 days at 10°C. 16 days at 19°C. Longer life at low temperature does not mean higher reproduction because activity, mating, and oviposition rates decline as temperature decreases. Females live longer than males in many studies, but the result varies with food, temperature, mating, and strain.

sectionTitle: Effect of Temperature on the Moth
text: Temperature affects: Activity. Flight. Mate searching. Pheromone release. Pheromone response. Mating. Egg laying. Longevity. Fecundity. Survival. Demographic models indicate that a range close to: 21–23°C Provides favorable conditions for survival of immature stages and female fecundity. The optimum temperature for oviposition has been estimated at approximately: 22.8°C. Females can lay eggs over a wider thermal range, but reproductive performance is not equal at all temperatures. Low Temperature It leads to: Reduced activity and flight. Delayed mating. Lower daily oviposition rate. Increased adult longevity. Slower population growth. Moderately High Temperature It leads to: Increased metabolic activity. Faster mating and oviposition. Shorter lifespan. Faster generation development. Extreme Heat Exposure to very high temperatures leads to: Reduced survival. Shortened adult lifespan. Disrupted fecundity. Reduced sperm efficiency. Reduced egg laying. Increased oxidative stress. A heat-stress study showed that exposure to: 40°C for more than three hours Significantly reduced adult survival.

Dense foliage creates a more humid microclimate with less exposure to sunlight and wind. Moths use this environment as a daytime refuge. Severe desiccation leads to: Increased water loss. Shortened adult lifespan. Reduced activity. Shorter oviposition period. Movement of moths into shaded sites. Increased mortality when combined with high temperature. High humidity does not always mean higher moth abundance because humidity interacts with temperature, rainfall, ventilation, and entomopathogenic diseases.

sectionTitle: Effect of Light and Ultraviolet Radiation
text: Moth behavior is affected by the alternation of light and darkness. Reproductive and locomotor activity increase during darkness or low-light periods. Ultraviolet radiation also affects insect physiology. Recent experiments showed that UV exposure reduced: Adult longevity. Female egg-laying capacity under severe exposure. Activity of some antioxidant enzymes. However, laboratory results based on concentrated radiation sources cannot be converted directly into field doses because natural exposure varies according to: Canopy cover. Time of day. Season. Glass or plastic used in greenhouses. Moth hiding site.

Wind affects moth movement in two ways: It may assist passive dispersal under some conditions. It may interfere with flight and female location when strong. Wind may push moths toward particular field edges or sectors, sometimes explaining uneven trap catches. Heavy rainfall may: Temporarily reduce activity. Knock some moths down. Interfere with pheromone release and dispersal. Drive insects into hiding. Reduce flight during wet periods. However, rainfall does not eliminate the pest population because eggs, larvae, and pupae occur in protected sites.

The adult insect is exposed to insecticides mainly through: Contact with spray droplets. Walking over treated surfaces. Inhalation of some volatile materials. Feeding on contaminated sources. Exposure to insecticide residues on leaves. Moths may die after treatment, but targeting adults alone is insufficient because the field generally contains: Eggs. Larvae inside mines. Pupae. New moths immigrating from outside. A female can lay a large proportion of its eggs during the first days of its life. Therefore, it may lay eggs before dying if insecticidal action is delayed. The efficacy of insecticides against moths is affected by: Active ingredient. Speed of action. Coverage. Timing relative to adult activity. Age of the insect. Resistance. Temperature. Exposure to sunlight. Repeated use of the same IRAC group.

sectionTitle: Natural Enemies of the Adult Insect
text: Adult moths are preyed upon by generalist predators such as: Spiders. Some predatory bugs. Nocturnal predatory insects. Small birds under some conditions. Organisms feeding on moths near the soil surface. Adult insects may also become infected with some entomopathogenic fungi when humidity and contact are sufficient. However, the adult stage is mobile and capable of flight, so its exposure to natural enemies differs from the relatively stationary egg and larval stages.

sectionTitle: Distinguishing the Adult from Similar Moths
text: Color and size alone cannot be used to identify Tuta absoluta because it is externally similar to other small moths. Species or groups with which it may be confused include: Phthorimaea operculella. Keiferia lycopersicella. Symmetrischema spp. Other species in the family Gelechiidae. Difference from Potato Tuber Moth Both species: Are small. Are gray or brown. Have narrow wings. Belong to the genus Phthorimaea under modern classification. Are associated with the family Solanaceae. Scale and spot patterns may differ, but reliable diagnosis depends on: Genitalia. Host and type of damage. Species-specific pheromone trap. Molecular analysis. Difference from Tomato Pinworm The two insects are similar in: Size. Color. Wing shape. Infestation of tomato. Membership in Gelechiidae. Therefore, separation requires diagnostic expertise and examination of the genitalia.

Pheromone traps simulate the sexual odor released by the female. The trap primarily attracts: Male Tuta absoluta. They are used for: Early detection. Determining the beginning of moth activity. Monitoring population trends. Identifying periods of increase and decline. Comparing different field areas. Supporting intervention decisions. Evaluating control programs. Mass trapping when used at suitable densities. However, the number of captured males does not equal: Number of females. Number of eggs. Number of larvae. Percentage of infested fruits. Trap catches may be low while larvae remain inside plants, or high because males are immigrating from a neighboring field. Therefore, traps must be interpreted together with: Egg inspection. Recent mines. Live larvae. Infested growing points. Fruit infestation. Condition of neighboring fields.

Mating disruption depends on releasing a quantity of synthetic pheromone throughout the production area so that the air becomes saturated with multiple chemical signals. This makes it difficult for: Males to locate females. Males to reach females. Mating to occur. Fertilized eggs to be produced. This method performs better when: Applied early. Initial pest density is low. A continuous area is treated. The greenhouse is well sealed or protected. Immigration of mated females from outside is prevented. Dispenser density is maintained. No severe neighboring outbreaks are present. Mating disruption does not kill eggs or larvae already present and must therefore be integrated with other components of integrated pest management.

Mass trapping increases trap density in order to remove a large proportion of males. Reducing male numbers decreases mating probability, particularly when the initial pest density is low. Its efficacy declines when: Infestation is severe. Mated females immigrate from outside. Old fields are nearby. Trap numbers are insufficient. The lure has expired. Traps are not maintained. Distribution is uneven. Traps are not a substitute for residue removal, plant inspection, biological control, and selective chemical control.

Adult insects are collected from: Pheromone traps. Plants. Greenhouse walls. Packages. Packing stations. Pupae reared until adult emergence. When collecting a specimen for diagnosis: 1. Do not hold the moth by the wings. 2. Use a fine brush or soft forceps. 3. Keep the specimen away from moisture. 4. Prevent several moths from rubbing against one another. 5. Record trap data, location, and date. 6. Record the host plant. 7. Preserve the specimen according to morphological or molecular examination requirements. Moths in sticky traps may be damaged by: Adhesive material. Loss of scales. Breakage of legs. Sticking of wings. Body decomposition when traps are left for long periods. An adult reared from a larva or pupa inside a clean container is preferable when genital examination is required.

External morphology is examined with a hand lens or stereomicroscope while recording: Body length. Forewing length. Wing color. Number and distribution of dark spots. Shape of the labial palpi. Shape of the antennae. Fringed wing margins. Abdominal form. Condition of scales. Host or collection site. A match with a single characteristic is not sufficient to confirm the species, especially in areas where related solanaceous moths occur.

sectionTitle: Applied Importance of the Adult Insect
text: The importance of the moth in integrated pest management is represented by the following: Early Warning Males appear in traps before severe damage is observed, allowing inspection intensity to increase. Identifying the Source of Immigration A sudden increase at field edges may reveal movement of moths from a nearby old field. Predicting Egg Laying Increased male activity in the presence of females indicates the possibility of a new wave of eggs and larvae. Evaluating Agricultural Sanitation Continued trap catches after harvest indicate continued moth emergence from pupae or immigration from residues and neighboring fields. Mating Disruption The moth is the direct target of pheromone dispensers. Mass Trapping Traps reduce male numbers when used at suitable densities. Preventing Spread Cleaning packages, vehicles, and packing stations helps prevent movement of moths and pupae into new areas.

sectionTitle: Common Errors in Interpreting the Adult Stage
text: Error One: The Moth Is the Stage That Bores into the Fruit The larva mines plant tissues, whereas the moth lays eggs. Error Two: Failure to See Moths Means There Is No Infestation Moths hide during the day, and larvae may remain inside the plant even when moth activity is low. Error Three: Every Small Gray Moth Is Tuta Similar species occur, and the moth must be linked to damage, pheromone response, and diagnostic examination. Error Four: Trap Catch Equals the Number of Insects per Feddan The trap measures male activity around it and is affected by location, wind, lure performance, and plant density. Error Five: Low Trap Catch Means Complete Control Success Male catches may decline while eggs, larvae, and pupae remain in the field. Error Six: Traps Attract Mated Females Conventional sex-pheromone traps primarily attract males and do not remove females present in the field. Error Seven: Replacing the Lure Alone Is Sufficient When the Trap Is Full The trap also requires cleaning or replacement of the adhesive surface and adjustment of height and location.

sectionTitle: Reference Table for the Adult Insect
text: Characteristic Description Name Tuta absoluta moth Stage causing direct damage? No Function Mating, egg laying, and dispersal Body length Usually approximately 6–7 mm Forewing length Approximately 4.2–5.0 mm Wingspan Approximately 8–12 mm Color Silvery gray to grayish brown Forewings Narrow and mottled with dark spots Hindwings Pale with long marginal fringes Antennae Long and filiform Labial palpi Long and upturned Legs Slender and banded with pale and dark scales Resting position Wings folded over the body Activity Mainly crepuscular and nocturnal Larger sex Female on average Pheromone source Female Sex captured by pheromone Mainly male Peak oviposition First days after mating Common field longevity Several days to approximately two weeks Principal monitoring method Pheromone traps Confirmatory diagnosis Genitalia or molecular analysis

The adult of Tuta absoluta is a small moth whose body length is usually approximately 6–7 mm and whose forewing length ranges from 4.2 to 5.0 mm. Its coloration is silvery gray or grayish brown, with dark and pale scales and small black spots on the forewings. The hindwings are lighter and bear long marginal fringes. The moth has long filiform antennae, upturned labial palpi, and three pairs of slender legs. It folds the wings over the body at rest, giving it a long, narrow form that assists camouflage. Females are larger and fuller than males on average, but accurate sex determination cannot be made from size alone. Confirmatory diagnosis of sex and species depends on the abdominal tip, genitalia, and microscopic or molecular examination. Moths are most active during evening and night and hide during the day within foliage, crop residues, and shaded sites. The female releases a sex pheromone that attracts males. This is the scientific basis of pheromone traps, mating disruption, and mass trapping. The female lays the largest proportion of its eggs during the first days after mating, and its lifetime egg production may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions. The moth causes no direct damage to the plant, but its economic importance is substantial because it moves infestation among plants and fields and produces the next generation of mining larvae. The integrated pest-management program therefore targets the adult stage through: Pheromone traps. Mating disruption. Mass trapping. Removal of crop residues. Prevention of moth migration from old fields. Cleaning packages and packing stations. Linking trap catches to inspection of eggs, larvae, and fruits. References
